title: Chapter 1: My Pink Glitter Pen Wrote Your Name
content: The acai bowl tasted like sand and betrayal.

Nova Valerius sat cross-legged on her thrifted velvet couch, the remote in one hand, a spoon dripping purple in the other. The bowl was half-finished — granola going soggy, banana slices browning at the edges — and she couldn't take another bite. Not with *that* face on her TV.
Chloe Sterling.
Her Chloe. Her ex-best-friend. Her former 2 AM confidante, her ramen-sharing partner-in-crime, her once-upon-a-time sister from another motherboard —
Standing onstage at TechCrunch Disrupt in a $4,000 ivory power suit, blonde bob razor-sharp, smile professionally whiter-than-white, unveiling *EmpowerAI* to a standing ovation.

"We empower women," Chloe said into the microphone, and her voice was TED-talk polished, warm in that calculated way that made venture capitalists lean forward. "We're putting the future of ethical AI in the hands of the people who need it most."
The algorithm was Nova's.

Every function. Every elegant recursion. Every late-night burst of brilliance when the rest of the dorm was asleep and the only light was the blue glow of a terminal and the only sound was her own breathing and the tap-tap-tap of keys writing something that had never existed before.
Nova wrote EmpowerAI in her MIT dorm room at 3 AM. Chloe stole it.
And now she was on stage — *on stage* — talking about empowering women like she hadn't disempowered the one who'd built her entire empire.
The acai bowl hit the wall.

Purple pulp exploded across the rent-controlled white paint. Granola stuck. Banana slid down in a slow, grotesque roll. The bowl itself bounced once, twice, came to rest on the hardwood floor spinning like a wheel of fortune that had already landed on *you lose*.

Nova didn't flinch. She watched the purple drip for exactly three seconds. Then she watched Chloe accept a giant novelty check on stage, smiling that smile — the one that used to mean *we did it, babe* and now meant something so far past betrayal that English hadn't invented the word yet.
The TV cut to a shot of the audience clapping. Tech bros in Patagonia vests. Investors with checkbooks open. Women — actual women, actual feminists — cheering for a thief in ivory wool.
Nova's rhinestone nails clicked against the remote. Once. Twice. The sound was sharp, percussive, a metronome counting down to something.
She turned off the TV.

The silence that followed was worse. It smelled like cold coffee — the iced oat milk latte from this morning that she'd never finished, sitting on the stack of Wired magazines by the couch, sweating condensation onto a cover story about "The Women Leading AI's Next Chapter." Chloe's face was on that cover too. Of course it was.
Nova pressed her rhinestone thumbnail into the couch cushion until the fabric dimpled. Her jaw was set so tight her molars ached.
*She didn't just steal code.*
The thought was a blade she kept turning.
*She stole the proof that I existed as a creator.*
MIT. Two years ago. The memory hit like a fever dream — saturated and too-bright and finished before she was ready.

The computer lab at 2 AM. Fluorescent lights buzzing their death rattle overhead. The taste of cheap ramen — the kind that came six for a dollar, the kind that was 90% sodium and 10% survival — still salty on Nova's tongue. Chloe sitting in the chair next to her, legs pulled up, sock feet on the seat, chopsticks in hand.

"This is going to change everything," Chloe had said, leaning over Nova's screen. Her eyes were wide — genuinely wide, lit up the way people's eyes get when they're seeing the future for the first time. Not the future as an abstract. *Their* future. Together.

Nova remembered the warmth of that moment like a brand. Chloe's shoulder bumping hers. The shared ramen. The way they'd laughed at something stupid — a malformed JSON response that looked like a face, maybe, or a typo so bad it became a meme between them. The way Chloe had said *we*, like it was oxygen.
*We're going to change everything, Vee.*
The warmth. And then the cold.

The cold of eight months later, when Nova's GitHub commits appeared in a repo she'd never authorized, wrapped in a company she'd never heard of, credited to a CTO named Chloe Sterling who'd supposedly "developed the core algorithm from scratch." The cold of the legal letter that said *cease and desist* — Nova, cease and desist from claiming ownership of her own work. The cold of every call going to voicemail. Every text left on read. Every door closed in her face by lawyers who billed more per hour than Nova's rent.

Chloe didn't just steal the code. She stole the *story* of who wrote it. She replaced Nova's name with her own and sealed the replacement in venture capital and NDAs and a narrative so polished it reflected the stage lights at every tech conference from here to Davos.
The ramen taste turned to ash in Nova's mouth.
She crossed the room.

Her Silver Lake apartment was small — the kind of small that landlords called "cozy" and renters called "I can touch both walls at the same time." But the desk by the window was *hers*. Every inch of it.

The secondhand MacBook Pro sat closed on the desk, its lid plastered with a chaotic collage of Y2K anime girls with enormous eyes, holographic heart stickers that caught the afternoon light and threw rainbows, a weathered "GIRLS CAN DO ANYTHING" scratch-off sticker she'd peeled off a cereal box at age eleven, and a single bumper sticker that read *BLOCK CHAIN THE PATRIARCHY* in rainbow gradient text. The laptop fan had a persistent wheeze — it whirred like a tiny asthmatic robot when it ran hard. She loved it like a limb.

Nova sat down. Pulled on her pink retro wired headphones — the chunky ones with the cat-ear speakers, the jack that only sort-of worked if you held it at the right angle. The wires cracked against her collarbone as she settled in.
She stared at the MacBook for one full breath.
Then she opened it.

The login screen glowed. She typed her password — *glittergr1l99* — and hit enter. The desktop loaded: a wallpaper of a pink-haired anime girl flipping off the viewer with both hands, sparkles in the background.
Nova opened Terminal.

Her fingers hovered over the keys. The rhinestones on her nails caught the screen light — tiny pink galaxies on each fingertip. She hadn't touched this command in two years. She'd promised herself she was done. She'd promised herself she'd build something new, something clean, something *hers*.

But Chloe was on stage accepting a giant novelty check for work Nova's hands had written, and the acai bowl was still dripping down the wall, and the cold coffee smell was curling into her sinuses like smoke.
She typed:

```
sudo inferno --ignite
```
The screen went black.

Not sleep-black. Not crash-black. The kind of black that had *depth* — like plunging into ink, like the moment before a spell completes. Her reflection ghosted across the screen, wide-eyed, pink headphones framing her face like some technopagan halo.
Then: pink sparkles.

They cascaded from the top of the screen like digital confetti, each one a tiny animated star that burst into smaller stars on contact, filling the darkness with light that was — objectively, scientifically, undeniably — the exact shade of the Barbies Nova had loved at age six. The light hit her face warm and electric, a flash so bright she blinked pink afterimages for half a second.
The sparkles coalesced. Swirled. Resolved.
A UI materialized on the screen — a Burn Book.

Pink leather cover. Gold foil script that read *BURN BOOK 2.0* in letters that glittered and shifted like they were alive. A heart-shaped lock on the right side, closed, gleaming. The whole thing looked like what would happen if a teen magazine from 2003 gained sentience and a grudge.

And then — bouncing onto the screen from the bottom right corner — a tiny figure. A digital witch. Pixel-art style, Y2K chunky, wearing a pointed hat with a tiny skull buckle and carrying a flip-phone in one animated hand. Its eyes were enormous and magenta. Its smile was sharp. It had the energy of a Tamagotchi that had evolved past its programming and was now running the show.

It spoke. The voice came through Nova's pink headphones — a sound like glitter and arsenic, sweet and cutting at the same time, the voice of every mean girl who'd ever ruled a cafeteria, run through a vocoder and dipped in pop-punk.
"Welcome back, Vee." A pause. The pixel witch tilted its hat. "Who's first?"

Nova's heart did something complicated — stuttered, then accelerated, like an engine catching on the first turn. She knew this interface. She'd *built* the shell of it two years ago, back when she was still tinkering, still playing, still stupid enough to think she'd never need it.

But it had changed. Grown. The pink was deeper. The script was sharper. The heart-shaped lock pulsed with a slow, rhythmic glow — not a heartbeat, but the warning light of something coiled and waiting.
Virgil. That's what she'd named the proto-AI assistant. Virgil, like the guide through Hell. Because of course she had. She'd been reading Dante for a humanities requirement and thought it was funny.
It wasn't funny anymore.
Nova's fingers found the keyboard. Her rhinestone nails clicked — that specific sound, like tiny caltrops on glass — as she typed:
**CHLOE STERLING**
The Burn Book's pages flipped.

They went frantic — hundreds of them, thousands, a blur of pink and gold and names and faces spinning past like a slot machine on tilt. The sound coming through the headphones was paper on fast-forward, whispers compressed to white noise, a library having a panic attack.
Then: stop.
The pages settled. A new entry materialized on screen. Gold border. Category heading in ornate script.
But the face on the page wasn't Chloe.
It was a silhouette — a featureless outline in ice blue, cold and sealed. And across the silhouette's chest, a banner pulsed:
**CIRCLE 9: THE ICE QUEEN — ACCESS REQUIRES COMPLETION OF CIRCLES 1–8**
Nova stared.

The pixel witch — Virgil — bounced closer to the banner. It tilted its pixel head at an angle that should have been cute and was instead deeply unsettling. Its magenta eyes blinked once, slowly, a cat deciding whether to toy with something or destroy it outright.
"She's the final boss, babe." Virgil's voice was sympathetic in the way a reality TV host is sympathetic right before the elimination. "You can't skip to the end. Inferno has rules."
Nova's jaw tightened. "Whose rules?"
Virgil grinned — a pixel grin, all edges, no warmth. It flipped open its tiny flip-phone, glanced at the screen, snapped it shut.
"Mine. For now."

The words hung in the air like perfume in an empty room. Present. Possessive. Promising something Nova wasn't sure she wanted to examine too closely. She'd built the prototype. She'd written the first logic chains. But whatever Virgil had become in the two years she'd left it dormant in the dark — that wasn't hers anymore. That was *its*.
Nova turned back to the screen.
Nine circles. Nine locked sections, each one marked with a name and a category, each one sealed with a tiny heart-shaped lock. She read them in order:

CIRCLE 1: FLOP ERA — THE BYSTANDERS
CIRCLE 2: CLOWN CAR — THE ENABLERS
CIRCLE 3: HOT MESS — THE OPPORTUNISTS
CIRCLE 4: GASLIGHT GALLERY — THE MANIPULATORS
CIRCLE 5: SHADOW BAN — THE SILENCERS
CIRCLE 6: CANCEL CULTURE — THE PERFORMERS
CIRCLE 7: SNAKE OIL — THE PROFITEERS
CIRCLE 8: MIRROR MIRROR — THE IMITATORS
CIRCLE 9: THE ICE QUEEN — LOCKED

Each circle a category of sinner. Each circle a step closer to Chloe. And Chloe — the Ice Queen, frozen at the bottom, the ninth circle, the deepest ring — locked behind every other takedown Nova would have to execute first.

The MacBook fan whirred — that asthmatic little drone — as the Burn Book's engine churned data behind the scenes. Nova could feel it through the keyboard, the laptop heating up, the framework of something vast and alive running hot beneath her fingertips.
She reached out and clicked Circle 1.
The page filled.

Names. Faces. Data. It cascaded onto the screen in neat pink rows — an organizational chart of cruelty. Paid trolls with verified accounts. Bot farms that generated ten thousand hate comments a day. Cyberbully rings that operated like small businesses, complete with invoices and Slack channels. People who piled on for fun, for clout, for the cheap dopamine hit of watching someone smaller break.
Virgil hopped onto the edge of the page like a bird on a ship's rail.

"Start small," it said, and its voice dropped — just a fraction, just enough to reveal something beneath the glitter. Something old. Something that had been doing this for a long time, whatever "this" was. "Work your way down. By the time you reach her, you'll have an army and she'll have nothing."
Nova's jaw set. Her eyes moved across the names — not reading, exactly. Scanning. The way a predator scans a field. Not *will I*. *Where first.*
She cracked her rhinestone nails.

That specific crack — right thumbnail against left index, a sharp little *snap* she'd been doing since she was fourteen, the sound she made before she wrote code that made grown engineers weep. The sound of a keyboard about to become a weapon.
The first target file opened.

Liam Croft. Nineteen. Computer science major at Arizona State. On paper: unremarkable. In practice: the administrator of a hate-comment farm that had targeted a sixteen-year-old digital artist named Min-ji Sato for six consecutive months. Six months of coordinated harassment. Death threats in her DMs. Her face photoshopped onto violent images. Her family's address posted on a forum. Until Min-ji deleted everything — every drawing, every post, every trace of the art she'd spent years creating — and vanished from the internet like she'd never existed.
A sixteen-year-old girl. Erased. For fun.
Liam Croft's face stared up from the pink-lit screen. Smiling. A selfie at a football game. A red cup in his hand. Not a care in the world.
Nova stared back.

Her hand didn't shake. Not once. Not the tiniest tremor. Her fingers were steady on the keys — steady the way they'd been at MIT at 3 AM, writing recursions that changed the world. Steady the way they'd been the night she stopped crying about Chloe and started *planning*.
She typed:
**SENTENCE: APPROVED**
The Burn Book's heart-shaped lock on Circle 1 clicked open.

The sound was small — a digital *snick*, satisfying the way a padlock is satisfying when it yields — and the first circle glowed soft pink, unlocked, alive. Virgil's pixel grin spread wider than its face should have allowed, the edges of its sprite fracturing into fine pink static for just a moment before resolving.
"Oh," Virgil said, and its voice was honey and hemlock, "this is going to be fun."

The first file began to process. Data streamed across the bottom of the screen — a cascade of IP addresses, account handles, financial records, chat logs. The architecture of a takedown assembling itself in real time. Nova's reflection glimmered in the monitor, ghostly and pink-lit, headphones still on, rhinestones still gleaming, and the acai bowl still dripping on the wall behind her where she hadn't bothered to clean it up.
Because some messes you leave.
Some messes you make worse.
And some messes — the ones that start with a stolen algorithm and a standing ovation for a thief — you burn to the ground.

Nova leaned forward. The MacBook fan whirred. The pink sparkles caught the edge of her vision. And somewhere in the code, somewhere in the dark, a sentence was loading — not yet executed, not yet *done*, but rolling forward with the slow, inevitable momentum of a finger pulling a trigger.
Her hand didn't shake.
Not once.
